title: 第 14 節
en: Lastly, great reverence is paid to the spirits of the departed2. The bones of dead people also are frequently preserved and treated with religious respect. The Ashantis have a word klas, which means the life of man. If used as a masculine, it stands for the voice that tempts man to evil ; if used in the feminine, it is the voice that persuades us to keep aloof from evil. Lastly, kla is the tutelary genius of a person who can be brought near by witchcraft, and expects sacrifices for the protection which he grants. When a man dies, his kla becomes sisa, and a sisa may be born again.
Many-sidedness of African religion.

Now I ask, is so many-sided a religion to be classed simply as African fetish- worship 1 Do we not find almost every ingredient of other religions 1 Kolle, 'African Literature,' 145. 2 Waitz, ii. 181.
3 'Baseler Missions-Magazin,' 1856, ii. 134, 139; Waitz, ii. p. 182.

IS FETISHISM A PRIMITIVE FORM OF RELIGION? 117 in the little which we know at present of the faith and worship of the negro ? Is there the slightest evidence to show that there ever was a time when these negroes were fetish-worshippers only, and nothing else 1 Does not all our evidence point rather in the opposite direction, viz. that fetishism was a parasitical development, intelligible with certain antecedents, but never as an original impulse of the human heart ?

What is, from a psychological point of view, the really difficult problem is, how to reconcile the rational and even exalted religious opinions, traces of which we discovered among many of the negro tribes, with the coarse forms of fetish- worship. We must remember, however, that every religion is a compromise between the wise and the foolish, the old and the young, and that the higher the human mind soars in its search after divine ideals, the more inevitable the symbolical representations, which are required for children and for the majority of people, incapable of realising sublime and subtle- abstrac tions.

Much, no doubt, may be said in explanation, even in excuse of fetishism, under all its forms and dis guises. It often assists our weakness, it often reminds us of our duties, it often may lead our thoughts from material objects to spiritual visions, it often comforts us when nothing else will give us peace. It is often said to be so harmless, that it is difficult to see why it should have been so fiercely reprobated by some of the wisest teachers of man kind. It may have seemed strange to many o£ us, that among the ten Commandments which were to set forth, in the shortest possible form, the highest, 118 LECTURE II.

the most essential duties of man, the second place should be assigned to a prohibition of any kind of images. * Thou shalt not make to thyself any graven image, nor the likeness of anything that is in heaven above, or in the earth beneath, or in the waters under the earth : thou shalt not bow down to them, nor worship them.'

Let those who wish to understand the hidden wisdom of these words, study the history of ancient religions. Let them read the descriptions of re ligious festivals in Africa, in America, and Australia, let them witness also the pomp and display in some of our own Christian churches and cathedrals. No arguments can prove that there is anything very wrong in all these outward signs and symbols. To many people, we know, they are even a help and comfort. But history is sometimes a stronger and sterner teacher than argument, and one of the lessons which the history of religions certainly teaches is this, that the curse pronounced against those who would change the invisible into the visible, the spiritual into the material, the divine into the human, the infinite into the finite, has come true in every nation on earth. We may consider ourselves safe against the fetish-worship of the poor negro ; but there are few of us, if any, who have not their own fetishes, or their own idols, whether in their churches, or in their hearts.
The results at which we have arrived, after examining the numerous works on fetishism from the days of De Brosses to our own time, may be summed up under four heads : —

i. The meaning of the word fetish (feitipo) has remained undefined from its first introduction, and IS FETISHISM A PRIMITIVE FORM OF RELIGION? 119 has by most writers been so much extended, that it may include almost every symbolical or imitative representation of religious objects.

2. Among people who have a history, we find that everything which falls under the category of fetish, points to historical and psychological antecedents. We are therefore not justified in supposing that it has been otherwise among people, whose religious development happens to be unknown and inacces sible to us.
3. There is no religion which has kept itself entirely free from fetishism.
4. There is no religion which consists entirely of fetishism.
Supposed psychological necessity of fetishism.

Thus I thought I had sufficiently determined the position which I hold with regard to the theory of a universal primeval fetishism, or at all events to have made it clear, that the facts of fetish-worship as hitherto known to us, can in no wise solve the question of the natural origin of religion.

The objection has, however, been raised by those who cling to fetishism, or at least to the Comtian theory of fetishism, that these are after all facts only, and that a complete and far more formidable theory has to be encountered before it could be admitted that the first impulse to religion proceeded from an incipient perception of the infinite pressing upon us through the great phenomena of nature, and not from sentiments of surprise or fear called forth by such finite things as shells, stones, or bones, — that is to say, by fetishes.
We are told that whatever the facts may be 120 LECTURE II.

which, after all, by mere accident, are still within our reach, as bearing witness to the earliest phases of religious thought, there must have been a time, whether in historic or prehistoric periods, whether during the formation of quaternary or tertiary strata, when man worshipped stocks and stones, and nothing else.

I am far from saying that under certain circum stances mere argumentative reasoning may not be as powerful as historical evidence ; still I thought I had done enough by showing how the very tribes who were represented to us as living instances of fetishworship possessed religious ideas of a simplicity and, sometimes, of a sublimity such as we look for in vain even in Homer and Hesiod. Facts had been collected to support a theory, nay had confessedly given the first impulse to a theory, and that theory is to remain, although the facts have vanished, or have at all events assumed a very different aspect. However, as it is dangerous to leave any fortress in our rear, it may be expedient to reply to this view of fetishism also, though in as few words as possible.

It may be taken for granted that those who hold the theory that religion must everywhere have taken its origin from fetishism, take fetish in the sense of casual objects which, for some reason or other, or it may be for no reason at all, were considered as endowed with exceptional powers, and gradually raised to the dignity of spirits or gods. They could not hold the other view, that a fetish was, from the beginning, an emblem or symbol only, an outward sign or token of some power previously known, which power, originally distinct from the fetish, was afterwards believed to reside in it, and in course IS FETISHISM A PRIMITIVE FORM OF RELIGION1? 121 of time came to be identified with it. For in that case the real problem for those who study the growth of the human mind would be the origin and growth of that power, previously known, and afterwards sup posed to reside in a fetish. The real beginning of religious life would be there ; the fetish would repre sent a secondary stage only. Nor is it enough to say (with Professor Zeller1) that 'fancy or imagination personifies things without life and without reason as gods.' The real question is, Whence that imagina tion "? and whence, before all things, that unprovoked and unjustifiable predicate of God ?

The theory therefore of fetishism with which alone we have still to deal is this, that a worship of casual objects is and must be the first inevitable step in the development of religious ideas. Religion not only does begin, but must begin, we are told, with a contemplation of stones, shells, bones, and such like things, and from that stage only can it rise to the con ception of something else — of powers, spirits, gods, or whatever else we like to call it.
Whence the supernatural predicate of a fetish?

Let us look this theory in the face. When travellers, ethnologists, and philosophers tell us that savage tribes look upon stones and bones and trees as their gods, what is it that startles us \ Not surely the stones, bones, or trees ; not the subjects, but that which is predicated of these subjects, viz. God. Stones, bones, and trees are ready at hand every where ; but what the student of the growth of the human mind wishes to know is, Whence their higher 1 ' Vortrage und Abhandlungen,' Zweite SamrnluDg, 1877, p. 32.

predicates ; or, let us say at once, whence their pre dicate God ? Here lies the whole problem. If a little child were to bring us his cat and say it was a vertebrate animal, the first thing that would strike us would surely be, How did the child ever hear of such a name as a vertebrate animal 1 If the fetishworshipper brings us a stone and says it is a god, our question is the same, Where did you ever hear of God, and what do you mean by such a name 1 It is curious to observe how little that difficulty seems to have been felt by writers on ancient religion.

Let us apply this to the ordinary theory of fetishism, and we shall see that the problem is really this : Can spirits or gods spring from stones ? Or, to put it more clearly, Can we understand how there should be a transition from the percept of a stone to the concept of a spirit or a god ?
Accidental origin of fetishism.

We are told that nothing is easier than this tran sition. But how \ We are asked1 to imagine a state of mind when man, as yet without any ideas beyond those supplied to him by his five senses, suddenly sees a glittering stone or a bright shell, picks it up as strange, keeps it as dear to himself, and then persuades himself that this stone is not a stone like other stones, that this shell is not a shell like other shells, but that it is endowed with extraordinary powers, which no other stone or shell ever possessed before. We are asked to suppose that possibly the stone was picked up in the morning, that the man who picked it up was engaged in a serious fight 1 Waltz, ii. 187.

IS FETISHISM A PRIMITIVE FORM OF RELIGION'? 123 during the day, that he came out of it victorious, and that he very naturally ascribed to the stone the secret of his success. He would afterwards, so we are told, have kept that stone for luck ; it might very likely have proved lucky more than once ; in fact, those stones only which proved lucky more than once would have had a chance of surviving as fetishes. It would then have been believed to possess some supernatural power, to be not a mere stone but some thing else, a powerful spirit, entitled to every honour and worship which the lucky possessor could bestow on it or on him.

This whole process, we are assured, is perfectly rational in its very irrationality. Nor do I deny it ; I only doubt whether it exhibits the irrationality of an uncultured mind. Is not the whole process of reasoning, as here described, far more in accordance with modern than with ancient and primitive thoughts 1 Nay, I ask, can we conceive it as possible except when men were already far advanced in their search after the infinite, and in full possession of those very concepts, the origin of which we want to have explained to us 1 Are savages like children?
zh: 最後，人們對逝者的靈魂懷有極深的敬意。死者的骨骸也常被保存下來，並受到宗教性的尊崇。阿散蒂人有一個詞 *klas*，意指人的生命。若用作陽性，它代表引誘人作惡的聲音；若用作陰性，則為勸說我們遠離邪惡的聲音。最後，*kla* 是一個人的守護神靈，可透過巫術召喚前來，並期待人們為其所賜予的保護獻上祭品。當人死去時，其 *kla* 便成為 *sisa*，而 *sisa* 可能再次轉生。
非洲宗教的多面性。
現在我要問，像這樣一個多面向的宗教，難道只能簡單歸類為非洲的拜物崇拜嗎？難道我們在其他宗教中找不到幾乎每一種成分嗎？¹ ¹ Kolle，《非洲文學》，145。² Waitz，卷二，181。
3 ‘Baseler Missions-Magazin,’ 1856, ii. 134, 139; Waitz, ii. p. 182.
在我們目前對黑人信仰與崇拜的有限認識中，是否真有證據顯示，曾有某個時期這些黑人僅是物神崇拜者，而別無其他？所有證據難道不都指向相反的方向，亦即物神崇拜是一種寄生性的發展，只有在某些前因條件下才可理解，但絕非人類心靈的原初衝動？
從心理學的觀點來看，真正困難的問題在於：如何調和我們在許多黑人部落中所發現的理性甚至崇高的宗教見解，與粗俗的拜物崇拜形式。然而，我們必須記住，每一種宗教都是智者與愚者、老者與少者之間的妥協；人類心靈在追尋神聖理想時飛得越高，那些為兒童及大多數無法領悟崇高而精微抽象概念的人們所必需的象徵性表現，就越是無可避免。

無疑地，對於拜物教（fetishism）在其所有形式與偽裝下的種種表現，我們可以說出許多辯解，甚至原諒之詞。它時常助長我們的軟弱，時常提醒我們的責任，時常引導我們的思想從物質對象轉向精神願景，也時常在其他事物無法給予我們平靜時安慰我們。人們常說它如此無害，以至於難以理解為何人類歷史上某些最睿智的導師會如此嚴厲地譴責它。在我們許多人看來，或許會覺得奇怪：在十誡（ten Commandments）——這份以最簡潔形式闡明最高準則的誡命中，竟會……
人類最根本的義務中，第二項應歸屬於禁止任何形式的偶像。*「不可為自己雕刻偶像，也不可仿造天上、地下或地底下水中的任何形像；不可跪拜它們，也不可事奉它們。」

願那些想要理解這些詞語中隱藏智慧的人，去研究古代宗教的歷史。讓他們閱讀非洲、美洲與澳洲宗教節慶的描述，也讓他們親眼目睹我們某些基督教會與主教座堂中的排場與展示。沒有任何論證能證明，所有這些外在的記號與象徵有何嚴重不妥。我們知道，對許多人而言，它們甚至是一種幫助與慰藉。然而，歷史有時是比論證更強硬、更嚴厲的導師；宗教史確實教導的教訓之一便是：那針對將不可見變為可見、將屬靈變為屬物質、將神聖變為屬人、將無限者變為有限者之人的咒詛，已在世上每個民族中應驗。我們或許自認對可憐黑人的拜物教（fetish-worship）免疫；但我們之中，幾乎沒有人——如果有的話——沒有自己的物神（fetishes）或自己的偶像（idols），無論是在他們的教堂裡，還是在他們的心中。
在檢視從德‧布羅斯時代至今為數眾多關於拜物教的著作後，我們所達到的結果可歸納為四點：—
i. 從「物神」（feitipo）一詞首次被引入以來，其意義始終未獲明確界定，而**物神崇拜是否為宗教的原始形式？** 119 大多數作者已將其涵義擴展到如此程度，以致幾乎可囊括所有宗教物件的象徵性或模仿性再現。
2. 在有歷史的民族中，我們發現所有歸屬於物神崇拜（fetish）範疇的事物，都指向歷史與心理上的前因。因此，我們沒有理由假設，在那些宗教發展恰好不為我們所知、也無法觸及的民族中，情況會有所不同。
3. 沒有任何宗教能完全免於拜物教（fetishism）的影響。
4. 沒有任何宗教完全由拜物教構成。

> 然而，在我們能夠理解物神崇拜（Fetishism）如何從人類心靈的原始狀態中必然產生之前，我們必須先釐清「物神崇拜」一詞的真正意涵。這個詞源自葡萄牙語 *feitiço*，意指「人造的」或「被施法的」，最初被歐洲航海家用來描述西非民族對某些物體所表現的宗教性崇敬。但若我們將此概念過度擴張，以至於涵蓋所有對物質對象的崇拜，那麼物神崇拜便會與圖騰崇拜（Totemism）、自然崇拜（Nature-worship）甚至偶像崇拜（Idolatry）混淆不清。因此，我們必須嚴格界定：物神崇拜並非單純地崇拜一塊石頭或一根木頭，而是崇拜被認為棲息於該物體中的某種靈性力量或存在。這種力量並非物體本身固有的屬性，而是被賦予或轉移進去的。正是這種「轉移」的過程，構成了物神崇拜的心理學關鍵。
因此，我自認已充分表明了我對普遍原始拜物教理論所持的立場，或者至少已清楚說明：迄今為止我們所知的拜物崇拜事實，絕無法解決宗教自然起源的問題。
然而，那些執著於拜物教，或至少是孔德式拜物理論的人，提出了反對意見。他們認為，上述內容終究只是事實；在我們承認宗教的最初衝動，是源於透過自然偉大現象向我們湧來的、對無限者的初步感知，而非源於諸如貝殼、石頭或骨頭這類有限事物——亦即「物神」——所引發的驚奇或恐懼情感之前，必須先面對一套更完整、也更難以應付的理論。
我們被告知，無論事實為何，120 第二講。
……那些純屬偶然留存至今、見證宗教思想最早期階段的遺蹟，無論是在歷史時期或史前時期，無論是在第四紀或第三紀地層形成期間，必定存在過一個時代，那時人類僅崇拜木石，別無他物。

我絕非斷言，在某些情況下，純粹的論證推理不可能與歷史證據同樣有力；然而，我本以為，透過指出那些被呈現為物神崇拜活生生例證的部落，竟擁有連荷馬與赫西俄德作品中都難以尋覓的、有時樸素、有時崇高的宗教觀念，便已足夠說明問題。事實曾被蒐集以支持某種理論，甚至坦承給予該理論最初的推動力——而這套理論仍要留存，儘管那些事實已然消失，或至少呈現出截然不同的面貌。不過，由於在後方留下任何據點都是危險的，因此，即便以最簡潔的篇幅，對這種物神崇拜的觀點作出回應，或許也是合宜之舉。

可以假定，那些主張宗教必然起源於物神崇拜（fetishism）的理論家，是將「物神」（fetish）理解為某種偶然的物體——這些物體因某種緣由（或根本毫無緣由）而被視為具有超凡力量，並逐漸被提升至精靈或神祇的尊位。他們不可能接受另一種觀點，即物神從一開始僅是一種象徵或符號，是某種先前已知力量的外在標記或表徵；這種力量原本與物神有別，後來才被認為寓居其中，並隨著時間推移與之合而為一。因為若果真如此，那麼研究人類心智發展者所面臨的真正問題，便在於那種先前已知、後來被認為寓居於物神中的力量，其起源與發展為何。宗教生活的真正起點應在於此；物神僅代表一個次級階段。此外，僅以（如策勒教授所言）「幻想或想像力將無生命、無理性的東西人格化為神祇」來解釋，亦是不夠的。真正的問題在於：那種想像力從何而來？而在此之前，那種毫無來由、無從證成的「神」之謂語，又從何而來？
因此，我們仍需處理的拜物教理論是：對偶然物件的崇拜，是且必須是宗教觀念發展中第一個無可避免的階段。據稱，宗教不僅始於，而且必須始於對石頭、貝殼、骨頭及諸如此類物件的凝視，唯有從此階段，它才能提升至對其他事物的概念——無論我們稱之為力量、靈體、神祇，或任何其他名稱。
物神崇拜的超自然謂語從何而來？
¹《演講與論文集》，第二輯，1877年，第32頁。
謂詞；或者，我們不妨直說：他們的謂詞「神」從何而來？此處正是問題的全部所在。倘若一個小孩帶著他的貓來對我們說：「這是脊椎動物」，我們首先想到的必然是：這孩子究竟是從哪裡聽來「脊椎動物」這個名稱的？如果一位拜物教徒帶著一塊石頭來對我們說：「這是神」，我們的疑問也相同：你究竟是從哪裡聽來「神」這個詞的？你用這個名稱究竟意指什麼？值得注意的是，古代宗教的研究者們似乎極少感受到這個難題。
讓我們將此應用於一般的物神崇拜理論，便會發現問題其實是：靈或神能從石頭中產生嗎？或者，更清楚地說，我們能否理解，從對一塊石頭的感知（percept）過渡到對一個靈或神的概念（concept），這一步是如何發生的？

> Fetishism, as we saw, is not a primitive form of religion, but a secondary corruption. It presupposes a previous stage of religious thought, in which the objects of nature were not yet degraded into mere receptacles of spirits, but were still held to be in themselves divine, or, at least, endowed with some mysterious power which called forth the religious awe of the worshipper. The transition from this higher conception to fetishism is gradual, and may be traced to a variety of causes, such as the loss of the original meaning of names, the confusion between the symbol and the thing symbolized, and the general tendency of the human mind to materialize its own conceptions. In many cases, a fetish is simply a relic of a more ancient worship, the original purport of which has been forgotten. A stone, a tree, a river, or a mountain, once adored as the abode of a god, or as the god himself, may, when its divine character is no longer understood, be retained as a charm or an amulet. Thus fetishism, far from being the starting-point of religious development, is rather a sign of its decline, a disease of language, as it were, which has obscured the original intuition of the Infinite. > 物神崇拜，如我們所見，並非宗教的原始形式，而是次生的墮落。它預設了一個更早的宗教思想階段，在此階段中，自然物體尚未被貶低為僅僅是神靈的容器，而是仍被視為本身即具有神性，或至少被賦予某種神秘力量，足以喚起崇拜者的宗教敬畏。從這種較高的概念過渡到物神崇拜是漸進的，並且可追溯至多種原因，例如名稱原始意義的喪失、象徵與被象徵物之間的混淆，以及人類心智將自身概念物質化的一般傾向。在許多情況下，一個物神僅僅是更古老崇拜的遺物，其原始意涵已被遺忘。一塊石頭、一棵樹、一條河流或一座山，曾作為神靈的居所或神靈本身而被崇拜，當其神聖性質不再被理解時，可能被保留為護符或辟邪物。因此，物神崇拜遠非宗教發展的起點，反而是其衰落的標誌，可謂一種「語言的疾病」，它遮蔽了對無限者的原始直觀。

我們被告知，這種轉變再簡單不過了。但我們被要求想像這樣一種心靈狀態：當一個人除了五官所提供給他的觀念之外尚無任何其他想法時，他忽然看見一塊閃亮的石頭或一枚光澤的貝殼，將其視為奇異之物撿起，珍藏於己，然後說服自己這塊石頭並非尋常石頭，這枚貝殼也非尋常貝殼，而是被賦予了其他石頭或貝殼從未擁有過的非凡力量。我們被要求設想，或許這塊石頭是在清晨被撿起的，而撿起它的人正投入一場激烈的戰鬥¹。 ¹ 瓦爾茲，卷二，第187頁。
白天時，他從中獲勝歸來，便很自然地將成功的秘訣歸功於那塊石頭。據說，他後來會把那塊石頭當作倖運物保存；它很可能不只一次證明瞭自己的幸運；事實上，只有那些不只一次證明自己幸運的石頭，才有機會作為物神（fetishes）留存下來。隨後，人們便會相信它擁有某種超自然的力量，不再只是一塊石頭，而是某種別的東西——一個強大的靈體，有權獲得那位幸運擁有者所能賦予它或祂的一切榮耀與崇拜。
我們確信，這整個過程在其極度非理性中，其實是完全理性的。我也不否認這點；我只是懷疑，這是否展現了未開化心靈的非理性。此處所描述的整個推理過程，難道不是更符合現代思想，而非古代與原始思想嗎？不僅如此，我要問，除非人類在追尋無限者的道路上已相當進步，並且充分掌握了那些我們亟需其起源解釋的概念，否則我們能設想這個過程是可能的嗎？野蠻人就像孩童嗎？
